mnemonics: PRIORITY POLLS INTERRUPTS (P.P.I.): Priority determines who gets serviced first when Polling or using Interrupts.
D.M.A. DIRECTS MEMORY ACCESS: Direct Memory Access lets devices talk directly to memory, bypassing the CPU.
keyPoints: Multiple devices can be serviced via Programmed I/O (Polling), Interrupt-Driven I/O, or Direct Memory Access (DMA).
Interrupt handling requires an interrupt controller to manage priorities and vector the CPU to the correct Interrupt Service Routine (ISR).
DMA transfers data between I/O devices and memory without continuous CPU involvement, freeing it for other tasks.
commonMistakes: Assuming all devices have equal priority; failing to implement proper priority handling can lead to data loss or system hangs.
Confusing vectored and non-vectored interrupts; a vectored interrupt provides the address of the ISR, while a non-vectored does not.
Forgetting to account for bus arbitration when using DMA; multiple devices (including the CPU) need a protocol to share the system bus.
